devanagari: सप्तमः स्कन्धःअथ द्वितीयोऽध्यायः । ’ श्रीमद्‍भागवतमहापुराणम्’ ग्रंथात ज्ञान वैराग्य व भक्ति यांनी युक्त निवृत्तीमार्ग प्रतिपादन केलेला आह...
english_devnagari: saptamaḥ skandhaḥatha dvitīyo'dhyāyaḥ’ śrīmad‍bhāgavatamahāpurāṇam’ graṃthāta jñāna vairāgya va bhakti yāṃnī yukta nivṛttīmārga pratipādana kelelā āhe aśā yā śrīmad‍bhāgavatāce bhaktine śravaṇa paṭhana āṇi nididhyāsana karaṇārā manuṣya khātrīne vaikuṃṭhalokālā prāpta hotohiraṇyākṣācyā vadhānaṃtara hiraṇyakaśipūkaḍuna mātā va kuṃṭuṃbiyāṃce sāṃtvananārada uvācabhrātaryevaṃ vinihate hariṇā kroḍamūrtināhiraṇyakaśipaṃ rājana paryaṃtapyadruṣā śucā
translation:
Nārada narrated:
When Hari, who had assumed the form of a boar, thus killed his brother Hiraṇyākṣa (out of partiality to gods), Hiraṇyakaśipu was agitated with wrath and grief, Oh King.

devanagari: आह चेदं रुषा घुर्णः सन्दष्ट दशनच्छदः । कोपोज्वलदभ्यां चक्षुभ्यां निरीक्षन ध्रुममम्बरम ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: āha cedaṃ ruṣā ghurṇaḥ sandaṣṭa daśanacchadaḥkopojvaladabhyāṃ cakṣubhyāṃ nirīkṣana dhrumamambarama
translation: Trembling with rage, biting his lips, and with eyes fiercely blazing with anger, he looked at the sky which appeared full of smoke; he spoke thus.

devanagari: करालदंष्ट्रोग्रदृष्ट्या दुष्प्रेक्ष्यभ्रुकुटीमुखः । शुलमुद्यम्य सदसि दानवानिदमब्रवीत ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: karāladaṃṣṭrogradṛṣṭyā duṣprekṣyabhrukuṭīmukhaḥśulamudyamya sadasi dānavānidamabravīta
translation: Looking fierce with his terrible big teeth, frowning look, and terrific looking face with knit eyebrows, he raised his trident in the assembly of Dānavas and addressed them as follows.

devanagari: भो भो दानवदैतेयाः द्विमूर्धस्त्रक्ष शम्बर । शतबाहो हयग्रीव नमुचे पाक इल्वल ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: bho bho dānavadaiteyāḥ dvimūrdhastrakṣa śambaraśatabāho hayagrīva namuce pāka ilvala
translation: “Oh Dānavas and Daityas, Oh Dvimūrdhan (two-headed demon), Oh three-eyed one, Śambara, hundred-armed one, horse-headed one, Namuci, Pāka, Ilvala, Vipracitti, Puloman, Śakuna and others! Listen to my words and then immediately execute them. Do not delay.

devanagari: विप्रचित्ते मम वचः पुलोमन शकुनादयः । श्रृणुतानन्तरं सर्व क्रियतामाशु मा चिरम ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: vipracitte mama vacaḥ pulomana śakunādayaḥśrṛṇutānantaraṃ sarva kriyatāmāśu mā cirama
translation: “Oh Dānavas and Daityas, Oh Dvimūrdhan (two-headed demon), Oh three-eyed one, Śambara, hundred-armed one, horse-headed one, Namuci, Pāka, Ilvala, Vipracitti, Puloman, Śakuna and others! Listen to my words and then immediately execute them. Do not delay.

devanagari: सपत्नैर्घातितःक्षुद्रैर्भ्राता मे दयितः सुहृत । पार्ष्णिग्राहेण हरिणा समेनाप्युपधावनैः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: sapatnairghātitaḥkṣudrairbhrātā me dayitaḥ suhṛtapārṣṇigrāheṇa hariṇā samenāpyupadhāvanaiḥ
translation: My beloved brother was killed by insignificant enemies through Hari who, though impartial, sided with them through their services (to Him).

devanagari: तस्य त्यक्तस्वभावस्य घृणेर्मायावनौकसः । भजन्तं भजमानस्य बालस्येवास्थिरात्मनः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya tyaktasvabhāvasya ghṛṇermāyāvanaukasaḥbhajantaṃ bhajamānasya bālasyevāsthirātmanaḥ
translation: He (Viṣṇu) was originally pure and impartial but through his assumption of the form of a boar by his Māyā, he has fallen away from his original nature. Like a child, he sides with him who serves him. His mind is unstable.

devanagari: मच्छूलाभिन्नग्रीवस्य भुरिणा रुधिरेण वै । रुधिरप्रियं तर्पयिष्ये भ्रातरं मे गतव्यथः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: macchūlābhinnagrīvasya bhuriṇā rudhireṇa vairudhirapriyaṃ tarpayiṣye bhrātaraṃ me gatavyathaḥ
translation: I shall satisfy my brother who is thirsting for blood, with the profuse blood (of Viṣṇu) with his neck cut off by my trident, and thus I shall relieve my grief.

devanagari: तस्मिन कुटेऽहिते नष्टे कृत्तमूले वनस्पतौ । विटपा इव शुष्यन्ति विष्णुप्राणा दिवौकसः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmina kuṭe'hite naṣṭe kṛttamūle vanaspatauviṭapā iva śuṣyanti viṣṇuprāṇā divaukasaḥ
translation: When the crafty adversary (whose actions are inscrutable) is destroyed, the residents of heavens (gods) whose very life is Viṣṇu, will wither away like branches of a tree the roots of which are cut.

devanagari: तावद्यात भुवं युयं विप्रक्षत्रसमेधिताम । सुदयध्वं तपोयज्ञस्वाध्यायव्रतदानिनः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: tāvadyāta bhuvaṃ yuyaṃ viprakṣatrasamedhitāmasudayadhvaṃ tapoyajñasvādhyāyavratadāninaḥ
translation: (While I am accomplishing this), you immediately repair to the earth which has been made prosperous by Brāhmaṇas and Kṣattriyas. Slaughter down those who are engaged in austere penance, sacrifice, Vedic studies, observance of religious vows and acts of charity.

devanagari: विष्णूंर्द्विजक्रियामुलो यज्ञो धर्ममयः पुमान । देवर्षिपितृभुतानां धर्मस्य च परयणम ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: viṣṇūṃrdvijakriyāmulo yajño dharmamayaḥ pumānadevarṣipitṛbhutānāṃ dharmasya ca parayaṇama
translation: Viṣṇu owes his existence to (lit. has for his roots in) the religious performance of Brāhmaṇas, for he Himself is the sacrifice and personified righteousness. He is the ultimate resort of gods, sages, manes and beings.

devanagari: यत्र यत्र द्विजा गावो वेदा वर्णाश्रमः क्रियाः । तं तं जनपदं यात सन्दीपयत वृश्चत ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: yatra yatra dvijā gāvo vedā varṇāśramaḥ kriyāḥtaṃ taṃ janapadaṃ yāta sandīpayata vṛścata
translation: Repair to those regions wherein dwell the Brāhmaṇas, cows, (recitation of) Vedas, (proper observance of the duties of) Varṇas and Aśramas. Devastate that country with fire, and cut down (the trees etc. useful for sacrifice).”

devanagari: इति ते भुर्तुनिर्देशमादाय शिरसाऽदुताः । तथा प्रजानां कदनं विदधुः कदनप्रियाः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: iti te bhurtunirdeśamādāya śirasā'dutāḥtathā prajānāṃ kadanaṃ vidadhuḥ kadanapriyāḥ
translation: In this way, they respectfully accepted the order of their master, bending their heads. Being innately disposed to indulge in harassment of others, they wrought havoc among subjects (on the earth).

devanagari: पुरग्रामव्रजोद्यानक्षेत्रारामा श्रमाकरान । खेटवर्वटघोषांश्च ददहुः पत्तनानि च ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: puragrāmavrajodyānakṣetrārāmā śramākarānakheṭavarvaṭaghoṣāṃśca dadahuḥ pattanāni ca
translation: They set fire to towns, villages, cow-pens, gardens, parks and fields, hermitages and mines, farmers’ settlements, villages at the foot of mountains, cow-herd settlements and even capital cities.

devanagari: केचित्खनित्रैर्बिभिदुः सेतुप्राकारगोपुरान । आजीव्यांस्चिच्चिदुर्वृक्षान केचित्पशुरपाणयः । प्रादहन शरणान्यन्ये प्रजांना ज्वालितोल्मुकैः ॥...
english_devnagari: kecitkhanitrairbibhiduḥ setuprākāragopurānaājīvyāṃsciccidurvṛkṣāna kecitpaśurapāṇayaḥprādahana śaraṇānyanye prajāṃnā jvālitolmukaiḥ
translation: Some knocked down and dismantled with spades bridges, ramparts and towers. Some with an axe in hand cut down fruit-bearing trees (serving as food to others). Some set on fire the houses of the people with flaming torches.

devanagari: एवं विप्रकृते लोके दैत्येन्दरानुचरैर्मुहुः । दिवं देवाः परित्यज्य भुवि चेरुरलक्षिताः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ viprakṛte loke daityendarānucarairmuhuḥdivaṃ devāḥ parityajya bhuvi ceruralakṣitāḥ
translation: When people were thus constantly harassed by the followers of Hiraṇyakaśipu, the king of the demons, (sacrifices could not be performed and) gods (who starved due to nonreceipt of their share in sacrificial oblations) deserted heaven, and wandered over the earth unperceived.

devanagari: हिरण्यकशिपुर्भ्रातुः सम्परेतस्य दुःखितः । कृत्वा कटोदकादिनी भ्रातुपुत्रानसान्त्वयत ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: hiraṇyakaśipurbhrātuḥ samparetasya duḥkhitaḥkṛtvā kaṭodakādinī bhrātuputrānasāntvayata
translation: (Though himself deeply) aggrieved, Hiraṇyakaśipu offered oblations of water to the spirit of his deceased brother, and performed other funeral rites. He consoled the sons of his brothers, viz., Śakuni, Śambara, Dhṛṣṭa, Bhūta-Santāpana, Vṛka, Kālanābha, Mahānābha, Hariśmaśru, Utkaca.

devanagari: शकुनिं शम्बरं धृष्टं भूतसन्तापनं वृकम । कालनाभं महानाभं हरिश्मश्रुमथोत्कचम ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: śakuniṃ śambaraṃ dhṛṣṭaṃ bhūtasantāpanaṃ vṛkamakālanābhaṃ mahānābhaṃ hariśmaśrumathotkacama
translation: (Though himself deeply) aggrieved, Hiraṇyakaśipu offered oblations of water to the spirit of his deceased brother, and performed other funeral rites. He consoled the sons of his brothers, viz., Śakuni, Śambara, Dhṛṣṭa, Bhūta-Santāpana, Vṛka, Kālanābha, Mahānābha, Hariśmaśru, Utkaca.

devanagari: तन्मातरं रुषाभानुं दितिं च जननीं गिरा । श्‍लक्ष्णया देशकालज्ञ इदमाह जनेश्वरः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: tanmātaraṃ ruṣābhānuṃ ditiṃ ca jananīṃ girāś‍lakṣṇayā deśakālajña idamāha janeśvaraḥ
translation: Hiraṇyakaśipu who knew what was appropriate for a particular occasion and situation (lit. place and time), addressed the following in soothing words to his nephews (mentioned above) and to their mother (his sister-in-law) Ruṣābhānu and to his own mother Diti, Oh ruler of men.

devanagari: हिरण्यकशिपुरुवाच । अम्बाम्ब हे वधुः पुत्रा वीरं मार्हथ शोचितुम । रिपोरभिमुखे श्लाघ्यः शुराणां वध ईप्सितः ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: hiraṇyakaśipuruvācaambāmba he vadhuḥ putrā vīraṃ mārhatha śocitumariporabhimukhe ślāghyaḥ śurāṇāṃ vadha īpsitaḥ
translation:
Hiraṇyakaśipu said:
Mother dear! Oh daughter (-in law)! Oh sons! You ought not to lament (the death of) a hero. Praiseworthy and covetable is the death of the brave, while facing the enemy (on the battle-field).

devanagari: भुतानामिह संवाहः प्रपायामिव सुव्रते । दैवेनैकत्र नीतानामुन्नीतानं स्वकर्मभिः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: bhutānāmiha saṃvāhaḥ prapāyāmiva suvratedaivenaikatra nītānāmunnītānaṃ svakarmabhiḥ
translation: The sojourn of beings in this world is like the get- together of travellers in a shed of water-jars (kept on the roadside for travellers to drink), Oh virtuous lady. Similarly, it is by the Providence that creatures are severally brought and are separated by their respective karmas.

devanagari: नित्य आत्माव्ययःशुद्धः सर्वगः सर्वविप्तरः । धत्तेऽसावात्मनो लिंग मायया विसृजन्गुणान ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: nitya ātmāvyayaḥśuddhaḥ sarvagaḥ sarvaviptaraḥdhatte'sāvātmano liṃga māyayā visṛjanguṇāna
translation: [1] The Soul is eternal (deathless), immutable, pure, omnipresent, omniscient, the cause of everything, yet distinct from the body, (hence you should not lament for Hiraṇyākṣa thinking him to be dead, separate etc.). Through his Māyā, he creates guṇas (such as various species of bodies, pleasure, pain) and assumes various forms.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
As usual, Bhāgavata Candrikā sets forth the Viśiṣṭādvaita theory of the Soul, viz. Ātman that is jūva pervades the whole body that he occupies (sarvaga) through his knowledge. To enjoy the fruit of his karma (whether merit or sin), he activates the guṇas (e.g. Sattva). The association of and separation from the body of each jīva is due to his karma.
But Bhāgavata Candrikā does not explain the get-together and dispersal of several jīvas as required by the text of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa
Padaratnāvalī points out that the jīva’s relation with his body etc. is temporary, but with that of the Lord is constant. It is the Lord who bestows the jīva with the mind, senses etc. Hari is the real support of the jīva.

devanagari: यथा मभसा प्रचलता तरवोऽपि चला इव । चक्षुणा भ्राम्यमाणेन दृश्यते चलतीत भूः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: yathā mabhasā pracalatā taravo'pi calā ivacakṣuṇā bhrāmyamāṇena dṛśyate calatīta bhūḥ
translation: Just as even the (reflection of) trees (in water appears to) move when the waters move, or the earth appears to revolve when one’s eyes are swimming, the same way, when the mind gets agitated and conditioned by the guṇas, the Perfect Person appears similarly conditioned and invested with a material body, even though he is bodiless.

devanagari: एवं गुणैर्भ्राम्यमाणे मनस्यविकलः पुमान । याति तत्साम्य्ततां भद्रे ह्यालिंगोलिंगवानिव ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ guṇairbhrāmyamāṇe manasyavikalaḥ pumānayāti tatsāmytatāṃ bhadre hyāliṃgoliṃgavāniva
translation: Just as even the (reflection of) trees (in water appears to) move when the waters move, or the earth appears to revolve when one’s eyes are swimming, the same way, when the mind gets agitated and conditioned by the guṇas, the Perfect Person appears similarly conditioned and invested with a material body, even though he is bodiless.

devanagari: एष आत्मविपर्यासो ह्यालिंगेः लिंगभावना । एष प्रियाप्रिर्यैर्योगो वियोगः कर्मसंसृतिः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: eṣa ātmaviparyāso hyāliṃgeḥ liṃgabhāvanāeṣa priyāpriryairyogo viyogaḥ karmasaṃsṛtiḥ
translation: It is the perversity of the Soul to presume its identification with the material body which does not exist. By this identification and attachment, are caused one’s union with the undesirable and separation from the dear ones, and vice versa, karmas, and entrance into various species of beings, birth and death, sorrows of various kinds, lack of discernment and discrimination (between the matter and the spirit), brooding over the (objects of senses), forgetfulness about the discrimination between the Soul and the body.

devanagari: सम्भवश्च विनाशश्च शोकश्च विविधः स्मृतः । अविवेक्श्च चिन्ता च विवेकास्मृतिरेव च ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: sambhavaśca vināśaśca śokaśca vividhaḥ smṛtaḥavivekśca cintā ca vivekāsmṛtireva ca
translation: It is the perversity of the Soul to presume its identification with the material body which does not exist. By this identification and attachment, are caused one’s union with the undesirable and separation from the dear ones, and vice versa, karmas, and entrance into various species of beings, birth and death, sorrows of various kinds, lack of discernment and discrimination (between the matter and the spirit), brooding over the (objects of senses), forgetfulness about the discrimination between the Soul and the body.

devanagari: अत्राप्युदहरन्तीममितिहासं पुरातनम । यमस्य प्रेतबन्धुनां संवादं तं निबोधत ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: atrāpyudaharantīmamitihāsaṃ purātanamayamasya pretabandhunāṃ saṃvādaṃ taṃ nibodhata
translation: In this context, the wise people give as an illustration, the following ancient legend, in the form of a conversation between Yama (the god of death) and the kinsmen of a dead person. Listen to that dialogue carefully.

devanagari: उशीनरेष्वभुद्राचा सुयज्ञ इति विश्रुतः । सपत्नैर्निहतो युद्धे ज्ञातयस्तमुपासत ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: uśīnareṣvabhudrācā suyajña iti viśrutaḥsapatnairnihato yuddhe jñātayastamupāsata
translation: In the country called Uśīnara, there lived a famous king by name Suyajña. When he was killed in the battle by his enemies his kinsmen stood around him.

devanagari: विशीर्णरत्‍नकवचं विभ्रष्टाबह्रणस्रजम । शरनिर्भिन्नहृदयं शयानमसृगाविलम ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: viśīrṇarat‍nakavacaṃ vibhraṣṭābahraṇasrajamaśaranirbhinnahṛdayaṃ śayānamasṛgāvilama
translation: He lay on the battlefield with his armour studded with jewels shattered, his ornaments and wreaths of flowers fallen away from his body. His heart was pierced with arrows and his body lay weltering in blood, the hair of the head dishevelled and scattered. His eyes were blown and lips were bitten with rage. His lotus-like face was covered with dust, and his weapons and arms were cut into pieces.

devanagari: प्रकीर्णकेशं ध्वस्ताक्षं रभसा दष्टदच्छदम । रजः कुण्ठमुखाम्भोजं छिन्नायुधभुजं मॄधे ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: prakīrṇakeśaṃ dhvastākṣaṃ rabhasā daṣṭadacchadamarajaḥ kuṇṭhamukhāmbhojaṃ chinnāyudhabhujaṃ mṝdhe
translation: He lay on the battlefield with his armour studded with jewels shattered, his ornaments and wreaths of flowers fallen away from his body. His heart was pierced with arrows and his body lay weltering in blood, the hair of the head dishevelled and scattered. His eyes were blown and lips were bitten with rage. His lotus-like face was covered with dust, and his weapons and arms were cut into pieces.

devanagari: उशीनरेन्द्रं विधिना तथा कृतं पतिं महिष्यः प्रसमीक्ष्य दुःखिताः । हताः स्म नाथेति करैरुरो भृशं घ्नन्त्यो मुहुस्तत्पदयोरुपापतन ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: uśīnarendraṃ vidhinā tathā kṛtaṃ patiṃ mahiṣyaḥ prasamīkṣya duḥkhitāḥhatāḥ sma nātheti karairuro bhṛśaṃ ghnantyo muhustatpadayorupāpatana
translation: Observing their husband, the king of Uśinaras, reduced to that plight by Fate, the queens were deeply grieved. Screaming out ‘We are undone, Oh Lord’, they violently beat their breasts with their hands now and then, and fell at his feet.

devanagari: रुदत्य उच्चेर्दयितांडघ्रिपंकज सिचंन्त्य अस्त्रैः कुचकुडकुमारुणैः । विस्रस्तकेशाभरणाः शुचं नृणां सृजन्त्य आक्रन्दनया विलेपिरे ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: rudatya uccerdayitāṃḍaghripaṃkaja sicaṃntya astraiḥ kucakuḍakumāruṇaiḥvisrastakeśābharaṇāḥ śucaṃ nṛṇāṃ sṛjantya ākrandanayā vilepire
translation: Bewailing loudly and bathing the lotus-like feet of their (dead) husband with tears tinted reddish with saffron paste on their breasts, and their hair and ornaments dishevelled and scattered, they wailed so piteously and loudly as to cause heartrending grief in the hearts of the listening persons.

devanagari: अहो विधात्राकरुणेन नः प्रभो भवान प्रणीतो दृदगोचरां दशाम । उशीनराणामासि वृत्तिदः पुरा कृतोऽधुना येन शुचां विवर्धनः ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: aho vidhātrākaruṇena naḥ prabho bhavāna praṇīto dṛdagocarāṃ daśāmauśīnarāṇāmāsi vṛttidaḥ purā kṛto'dhunā yena śucāṃ vivardhanaḥ
translation: “Alas! Your majesty has been transported to a stage of existence beyond the range of our sight, by the merciless Destiny, Oh Lord. You who were once the giver of livelihood (and happiness) to the people of Uśīnara country, have now been made the cause of their increasing grief, by it.

devanagari: त्वया कृतज्ञेन वयं महीपते कथं विना स्याम सुहृत्तमेन ते । तत्रानुयानं तव वीर पादयोः शुश्रुषतीनां दिशं यत्र यास्यसि ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: tvayā kṛtajñena vayaṃ mahīpate kathaṃ vinā syāma suhṛttamena tetatrānuyānaṃ tava vīra pādayoḥ śuśruṣatīnāṃ diśaṃ yatra yāsyasi
translation: Oh Lord of the earth! How can we live without you, our best friend and appreciator of merits? Permit us who are desirous of serving your feet, to follow you wherever you will go (i.e. to die after you)

devanagari: एवं विलपतीनांवै परिगृह्या मृतं पतिम । अनिच्छतीनां निर्हारकार्कोऽस्तं संन्यवर्तत ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ vilapatīnāṃvai parigṛhyā mṛtaṃ patimaanicchatīnāṃ nirhārakārko'staṃ saṃnyavartata
translation: While they were lamenting thus clasping their dead husband, and unwilling to allow him to be removed for cremation, the Sun set in the sky.

devanagari: तत्र ह प्रेतबन्धुनामाश्रुत्य परिदेवितम । आह तान बालको भुत्वा यमः स्वय्मुपागतः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: tatra ha pretabandhunāmāśrutya paridevitamaāha tāna bālako bhutvā yamaḥ svaymupāgataḥ
translation: Having heard the wailings of the relatives of the dead (even while he was in his capital), Yama (the god of death) himself approached them, in the form of a boy, and spoke to them.

devanagari: यम उवाच । अहो अमीषां वयसाधिकांनी विपश्यतां लोकविधि विमोहः । यत्रगतस्तत्र गतं मनुष्यं स्वयं सधर्मा अपि शोचन्त्यपार्थम ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: yama uvācaaho amīṣāṃ vayasādhikāṃnī vipaśyatāṃ lokavidhi vimohaḥyatragatastatra gataṃ manuṣyaṃ svayaṃ sadharmā api śocantyapārthama
translation:
Yama said:
Oh! How astonishing is the folly of these elderly people who have been daily observing the course of the world (viz. the death of everyone who is born). Though themselves of the same (mortal) nature as that of the dead person, they bewail in vain the man who has returned to the (unmanifest) state from which he has come (originally).[2]
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Cf. avyaktādīni bhūtāni vyaktamadhyāni bhārata /
avyaktanidhanānyeva tatra kā paridevanā // B.G. 2.28.

devanagari: अहो वयं धन्यतमा यदत्र त्यक्ता पितृषां न विचिन्तयामः । अभक्ष्यमाणा अबला वृकादिभिः स रक्षिता रक्षति यो हि नर्भे ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: aho vayaṃ dhanyatamā yadatra tyaktā pitṛṣāṃ na vicintayāmaḥabhakṣyamāṇā abalā vṛkādibhiḥ sa rakṣitā rakṣati yo hi narbhe
translation: Oh! How blessed-most we are that though abandoned by parents in this world, we do not feel any anxiety. Weak, as we are, we are not devoured by (carnivorous animals like) wolves and others. (We feel confident that) he who protects us while (we were) in the womb, shall protect us (in this world).

devanagari: य इच्छयेशः सृजतीदमव्ययो य एव रक्षत्यवलुम्पते च यः । तस्याबलाः क्रीडनमाहुरेशितु श्चराचरं निग्रहसंग्रेहे प्रभुः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: ya icchayeśaḥ sṛjatīdamavyayo ya eva rakṣatyavalumpate ca yaḥtasyābalāḥ krīḍanamāhureśitu ścarācaraṃ nigrahasaṃgrehe prabhuḥ
translation: Oh ladies! The imperishable Lord, at his sweet will, creates, protects and destroys the universe. The wise people call the mobiles and immobiles as mere toys. It is the Lord alone, who is powerful (enough) to preserve and destroy it.

devanagari: पथि च्युतं तिष्ठति दिष्टरक्षितं गृहे स्थितं तद्विहतं विनश्यति । जीवत्यनाथोऽपि तदीक्षितो वने गृहेऽपि गुप्तोऽस्य हतो न जीवति ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: pathi cyutaṃ tiṣṭhati diṣṭarakṣitaṃ gṛhe sthitaṃ tadvihataṃ vinaśyatijīvatyanātho'pi tadīkṣito vane gṛhe'pi gupto'sya hato na jīvati
translation: Protected by the Lord, one survives even if abandoned by the road side. If ignored by Him, one meets death even while staying at home. A helpless creature can stay alive even in a jungle, if looked after by Him; while a person if destined by Him to die, shall never live even if protected at home (by the use of medicines, incantations etc.).

devanagari: भुतानि तैस्तैर्निजयोनिकर्मभिर्भवन्ति काले न भवन्ति सर्वशः । न तत्र हात्मा प्रकृतावपि स्थित स्तस्या गुणैरन्यतमो निबध्यते ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: bhutāni taistairnijayonikarmabhirbhavanti kāle na bhavanti sarvaśaḥna tatra hātmā prakṛtāvapi sthita stasyā guṇairanyatamo nibadhyate
translation: Bodies of all species (including those of gods, subhuman and human beings) come into existence and perish at a particular time according to the karmas depending on the subtle body. But the Soul though it inhabits the Prakṛti (the corporal body), is not affected by the attributes of the Prakṛti (body) as he is different from it.

devanagari: इदं शरीरं पुरुषस्य मोहजं यथा पृथग्भीतिकमीयते गृहम । यथौदकैः पार्थिवतैजसैर्जनः कालेनःजातो विकृतो विनश्यति ॥ ४२ ॥
english_devnagari: idaṃ śarīraṃ puruṣasya mohajaṃ yathā pṛthagbhītikamīyate gṛhamayathaudakaiḥ pārthivataijasairjanaḥ kālenaḥjāto vikṛto vinaśyati
translation: This corporal body of Puruṣa (the Soul or Spirit) is caused by delusion, and being made of bhūtas, is different from the Soul, just as a house is different from its dweller. Like bubbles constituted of atoms of water, pots made of earth, gold ornaments made of (solidified) fire, this body formed (out of elements) by Time, undergoes modification and perishes.

devanagari: यथानलो दारुषु भिन्न ईयते यथानिलो देहगतः पृथक स्थितः । यथा नभः सर्वगतं ग सज्जते तथा पुमान सर्वगुणाश्रयः परः ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: yathānalo dāruṣu bhinna īyate yathānilo dehagataḥ pṛthaka sthitaḥyathā nabhaḥ sarvagataṃ ga sajjate tathā pumāna sarvaguṇāśrayaḥ paraḥ
translation: Just as fire remaining latent in the pieces of wood is distinct from them, or the vital breath existing in the body remains different from it, or the sky pervading all things does not stick to anything, similarly the Soul, the supporter of all the guṇas, i.e. their product, e.g. the body, sense organs or who dwells in them, is distinct from them.[3]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
According to Padaratnāvalī verses 41-43 emphasize the distinction of Paramātman from Jīva. In 43, the presence of the Paramātman in the body is not the effect of delusion, and as such he is not affected by the guṇas.

devanagari: सुयज्ञो नन्वयं शेते मुढा यमनुशोचथ । यः श्रोता योऽनुवक्तेह स न दृश्येत कर्हिचित ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: suyajño nanvayaṃ śete muḍhā yamanuśocathayaḥ śrotā yo'nuvakteha sa na dṛśyeta karhicita
translation: You fools! This (body called) Suyajña whom you are lamenting, is lying here. He who heard you and then replied, could never be seen by you.

devanagari: न श्रोता नानुवक्तायं मुख्योऽप्यत्र महानसुः । यस्त्विहेन्द्रियवानात्मा स चान्यः प्राणदेहयोः ॥ ४५ ॥
english_devnagari: na śrotā nānuvaktāyaṃ mukhyo'pyatra mahānasuḥyastvihendriyavānātmā sa cānyaḥ prāṇadehayoḥ
translation: Not even the great vital air (mukhya-prāṇa) in the body which is called life, is the listener or the speaker. The principle, which is the master and controller of senses, is the real ātman, and it is distinct from the body and the life-breath mukhya prāṇa).

devanagari: भुतेन्द्रियमनोलिंगन देहानुच्चावचान विभूः । भजत्युत्सृजति ह्यान्यस्तच्चपि स्वेन तेजसा ॥ ४६ ॥
english_devnagari: bhutendriyamanoliṃgana dehānuccāvacāna vibhūḥbhajatyutsṛjati hyānyastaccapi svena tejasā
translation: This all-pervasive principle ātman, though distinct, assumes different bodies (celestial, human and sub-human), consisting of five elements (bhūtas), senses and mind, and regards himself as one with them; and by dint of his power and wisdom, he casts them off (as well).

devanagari: यावाल्लिंगन्वितो ह्यात्मा तावत कर्म निबन्धनम । ततो विपर्ययः क्लेशो मायायोगोऽनुवर्तते ॥ ४७ ॥
english_devnagari: yāvālliṃganvito hyātmā tāvata karma nibandhanamatato viparyayaḥ kleśo māyāyogo'nuvartate
translation: So long as its association with Māyā continues, the ātman identifies itself with the subtle body (liṅga-śarīra) and feels attachment for it. The bondage of karma holds sway over him. It leads to the perversion of the Soul, resulting into misery.

devanagari: वितथाभिनिवेशोऽयं यद गुणैष्वर्थदृग्वचः । यथा मनोरथः स्वप्नः सर्वमैन्द्रियकं मृषा ॥ ४८ ॥
english_devnagari: vitathābhiniveśo'yaṃ yada guṇaiṣvarthadṛgvacaḥyathā manorathaḥ svapnaḥ sarvamaindriyakaṃ mṛṣā
translation: It is a false notion and attachment to look upon and speak of (products of) guṇas (such as pleasure, pain) as real. Like our fanciful desires and dreams, all sense-experience is unreal.

devanagari: अथ नित्यमनित्यं वा नेह शोचन्ति तद्विदः । नान्यथा शक्यते कर्तृं स्वभावः शोचतामिति ॥ ४९ ॥
english_devnagari: atha nityamanityaṃ vā neha śocanti tadvidaḥnānyathā śakyate kartṛṃ svabhāvaḥ śocatāmiti
translation: Hence, the knowers of the reality do not grieve for what is eternal (the Soul) or what is transient (e.g. body, sense-organs, objects of pleasure). But the nature of those who are given to grieving, cannot be changed (as they have not imbibed the knowledge of reality).

devanagari: लुब्धको विपिने कश्चित्पक्षिणां निर्मितोऽन्तकः । वितत्य जालं विदधे तत्र तत्र प्रलोभयन ॥ ५० ॥
english_devnagari: lubdhako vipine kaścitpakṣiṇāṃ nirmito'ntakaḥvitatya jālaṃ vidadhe tatra tatra pralobhayana
translation: A fowler, created as the destroyer of birds (by God) spread his net in the forest, placing allurements at different places.

devanagari: कुलिंगमिथुनं तत्र विचरत्समदृश्यत । तयोः कुलिंगीं सहसा लुब्धकेन प्रलोभिता ॥ ५१ ॥
english_devnagari: kuliṃgamithunaṃ tatra vicaratsamadṛśyatatayoḥ kuliṃgīṃ sahasā lubdhakena pralobhitā
translation: A pair of Kuliṅga birds (sparrows) was seen flying over it. Out of them, the female sparrow was soon allured by the fowler.

devanagari: सासज्जत शिचस्तन्त्यां महिषी कालयन्त्रिता । कुलिंगस्तां तथाऽपन्नां निरीक्ष्य भृशदुःखितः । स्नेहादकल्पः कृपणः कृपणां पर्यदेवयतः ॥ ५२ ॥
english_devnagari: sāsajjata śicastantyāṃ mahiṣī kālayantritākuliṃgastāṃ tathā'pannāṃ nirīkṣya bhṛśaduḥkhitaḥsnehādakalpaḥ kṛpaṇaḥ kṛpaṇāṃ paryadevayataḥ
translation: The queen-bird, being dragged by Time (Destiny), was enmeshed in the net. Seeing her in that miserable condition, the male partner was extremely afflicted. The pitiable mate, being helpless (to rescue her and to control his own grief), lamented over his miserable companion, out of affection:

devanagari: अहो अकरुणो देवः स्त्रियाऽकरुणया विभुः । कृपणं मानुशोचन्त्त्या दीनया किं करिष्यति ॥ ५३ ॥
english_devnagari: aho akaruṇo devaḥ striyā'karuṇayā vibhuḥkṛpaṇaṃ mānuśocanttyā dīnayā kiṃ kariṣyati
translation: “Oh! What that merciless yet almighty Providence is going to do with my wife, who herself is pitiable and helpless in every respect, is lamenting for me.

devanagari: कामं नयतु मां देवः किमर्धेनात्मनो हि मे । दीनेन जीवता दुःखमनेन विधुरायुषा ॥ ५४ ॥
english_devnagari: kāmaṃ nayatu māṃ devaḥ kimardhenātmano hi medīnena jīvatā duḥkhamanena vidhurāyuṣā
translation: May the Deity be pleased to take me also. Of what use is to me the survival of the one (male) half leading a miserable life, after being separated from my better half?

devanagari: कथं त्वजातपक्षांस्तान मातृहीनाना बिभर्म्यहम । मन्दभाग्यः प्रतीक्षन्ते नीडे मे मातरं प्रजाः ॥ ५५ ॥
english_devnagari: kathaṃ tvajātapakṣāṃstāna mātṛhīnānā bibharmyahamamandabhāgyaḥ pratīkṣante nīḍe me mātaraṃ prajāḥ
translation: How can I maintain those unfledged motherless young ones? Those unfortunate children of mine must be waiting for the arrival of their mother.”

devanagari: एवं कुलिंग विलपन्तमारात प्रियवियोगातुरमश्रुकण्ठम । स एव तं शाकुनिकः शरेण विव्याध कालप्रहितो विलीनः ॥ ५६ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ kuliṃga vilapantamārāta priyaviyogāturamaśrukaṇṭhamasa eva taṃ śākunikaḥ śareṇa vivyādha kālaprahito vilīnaḥ
translation: While the male bird, who was grieved at the separation from his wife, was thus lamenting with his voice choked with tears, the fowler, as if impelled by fate, concealed himself close by, and hit the bird with an arrow.

devanagari: एवं युयमपश्यन्त्य आत्मापायमबुद्धयाः । नैनं प्राप्स्यथ शोचन्त्यः पतिं वर्षशतैरपि ॥ ५७ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ yuyamapaśyantya ātmāpāyamabuddhayāḥnainaṃ prāpsyatha śocantyaḥ patiṃ varṣaśatairapi
translation: So, you foolish ladies who do not foresee your own destruction (death) will never get back your husband, even if you bewail for hundreds of years.”

devanagari: हिरण्यकशिपुरुवाच । बाल एवं प्रवदति सर्वे विस्मितचेतसः । ज्ञातयो मेनिरे सर्वमनित्यमयथोत्थितम ॥ ५८ ॥
english_devnagari: hiraṇyakaśipuruvācabāla evaṃ pravadati sarve vismitacetasaḥjñātayo menire sarvamanityamayathotthitama
translation:
Hiraṇyakaśipu said:
While the child was thus admonishing, all the kinsmen (of Suyajña) became astonished, and realized that everything is transient and unreal.

devanagari: यम एतदुपाख्याय तत्रैवान्तरधीयत । ज्ञातयोऽपि सुयज्ञस्य चक्रुर्यत्साम्परयिकम ॥ ५९ ॥
english_devnagari: yama etadupākhyāya tatraivāntaradhīyatajñātayo'pi suyajñasya cakruryatsāmparayikama
translation: Having exhorted them thus, Yama also disappeared at that very spot. The relatives of Suyajña performed his funeral rites.

devanagari: ततः शोचत मा युयं परं चात्मानमेव च । क आत्मा कः परो वात्र स्वीयः पारक्य एव वा । स्वपराभिनिवेशेन विनाज्ञानेन देहिनाम ॥ ६० ॥
english_devnagari: tataḥ śocata mā yuyaṃ paraṃ cātmānameva caka ātmā kaḥ paro vātra svīyaḥ pārakya eva vāsvaparābhiniveśena vinājñānena dehināma
translation: Hence you need not grieve for yourself or for others. In the case of ignorant beings, the differentiation between one’s own self and another or what belongs to one’s own self and another, is due to the false notion of distinction between one’s own self from another (and the consequent attachment for one’s own body and property)

devanagari: नारद उवाच । इति दैत्यपतेर्वाक्यं दितिराकर्ण्य सस्नुषा । पुत्रशोकं क्षणात्यक्त्वा तत्त्वे चित्तमधारयत ॥ ६१ ॥
english_devnagari: nārada uvācaiti daityapatervākyaṃ ditirākarṇya sasnuṣāputraśokaṃ kṣaṇātyaktvā tattve cittamadhārayata
translation:
Nārada said:
Having heard the speech of the king of Daityas (Hiraṇyakaśipu) Diti, along with her daughter-in-law, immediately stopped sorrowing for her son and steadied her heart by fixing it on Reality.
[back to top]
